speaker: interviewer
text: Rita Finver-- F-I-N-V-E-R. This is roll number six.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came over the Saturday holiday to Spring Valley, and the uncle did offer that we could work there. But of course he was going away, and the hotel was closed in the winter time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came back to the Bronx. We stayed in the Bronx. Leo immediately went to work. He worked in the Fulton Market with eggs. And I immediately got a job in a hospital, even though I had no credentials. But there were two doctors, one from Bad Gastein and from the clinic, and one, the doctor that taught me in Russia and who, by that time, was back. He came to United States. He married an American lady. And he immediately came as a husband. So on their say so, I started to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And with my broken English, I was working, always in hospitals. I never worked at home. Once-- one week I worked at home because a friend told me, you're so foolish. You're working here for peanuts. You can make more money working at home. You will have what to eat. But I never liked it. And because I always stuck out in hospitals where I could grow in my experience, in my learning experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: So towards the end, after working in New York, first in Fulton Division in the Bronx, then I was working four years in Beth Israel Hospital, which was a teaching medical center, had a nursing school. I went there because I thought that perhaps through them I could get something, you know, some document.

speaker: interviewee
text: I immediately went to take a test. And with flying colors, without any problems, I did receive a practical nurses license. So I really worked in the hospital very frequently. I was not in charge on the floor, but I was in charge in the nursery, with a lot of writing in the charts, as a pract-- licensed practical nurse. And I was getting 25% of the salary than, let's say, the graduate nurses who were from-- there were two or three nurses from an accredited school in Poland. Otherwise-- and nurses from Germany also-- they had their documents. Anyhow, after that, I worked four years in Beth Israel Hospital. During that time, our first child was born and died in Beth Israel Hospital. She had some complications. A year later-- I was working even during my pregnancy. My reputation and my apparently my know-how was so extensive from the experience from during the war that I had no problem working. The problem was to get the proper salary and to get the accreditation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So later, our son was born. Our son was born in, in 19-- 1953, I believe. Tell me his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sheldon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sheldon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Exactly-- almost one year-- one year after our first child was born. Prior to that, I had-- I was pregnant. In the-- in Bad Gastein, I didn't want to have children. We felt that we should come and get independent on our own, not to depend on, on, on handouts. And later when we wanted to have, I was already 30 years old. So first I had an abortion. And then my first child died. And a year later Sheldon was, was born. Sheldon was born in Beth Israel Hospital. And Beth Israel is a good-- is up till today a good medical center. Of course, it's changed since then. I wor-- I worked there four years. After that we decided to go on a farm because we thought that being together, making a living, the family will be close knit. But as it turned out, the economic situation, by the time we came, it turned around. The entire situation turned around. Anyhow, I was working there in the hospital. And Leo was taking eggs to New York. And this is how we sort of were able to pay our debts on the farm. We didn't have enough money even to buy the property. But after 10 years, we left Vineland. We came back to New York. And again, in Vineland I worked in a hospital, and I had a very good reputation. When I came back to New York. I worked, again, in a hospital. And later on, in 1970, '70, the union that I was a member-- the unionized nurses and the unionized telephone operators-- they had a training and upgrading program. And that was one of my biggest breaks in life because they gave me 85% of my salary. They paid my book. I did not pay for any courses. I could choose whatever I want. And after two years, with my background of knowledge of practical nurses-- nursing, after two years, I did receive an associate degree. And while I was working before as a nurse in charge in Beth Abraham hospital-- this is a part of Montefiore-- and a lot of registered nurses were coming, and I was teaching them how to coordinate, how to write medication, how to count narcotics, how to admit a patient, and I was getting paid as a practical nurse. After the two years, I was put back on the floor, immediately on in charge of the floor with a lot of teaching of nurses aids. So I was as a registered nurse another 11 years. But the union who paid for me did not-- did not have RNs. As an RN, I was a member of New York Nurses State Association-- New York State Nurses Association. As it is, I'm getting a small pension from 1199. They were the most generous because the years that I was in school were incorporated. And when I worked in Beth Israel, at that time, they didn't have a union of all. They somehow find out, even though Beth Israel didn't have documents anymore, they invest-- someone liked me there because maybe they felt I'm a Holocaust survivor, and maybe he was, you know, the boss there, and he investigated. So I get a pension from 1199. And I get a pension from-- all together it's not much because it was a noncontributory pension. The pension is only a certain percentage of what I was making. Now in 1985, I retired from Beth Israel Hospital. Beth Israel was a geriatric facility connected with Montefiore Hospital. And from there, we came-- we-- a year later, we had a house in Jamaica, Queens. We sold the house, and we moved to, to Florida. Our son, in 1972, I believe, 1972, at the same time when I was in school, he was in Stony Brook. And he graduated Stony Brook. And for Stony Brook, he was accepted into science, into g-- it was microbiology, molecular biology. But really, it was the DNA replication and protein synthesis and, and how the genetic code with the-- how should I say it-- the doub-- the, the double helixes broken up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, this is the research he did. But somehow it took him awhile until he got the PhD. He got the PhD in 1980. So he was as long in his PhD program as, let's say, someone else was in a medical school, including, including, let's say, being a intern and a-- Where is he now?

speaker: interviewee
text: First he was in Philadelphia. And he was on grants from National Institute of Health. He was on very, very prominent in science, in Wistar Institute, which is across from University of Pennsylvania, where there were 400 scientists doing all kind of research. But-- and then he was in Jefferson University and Temple University, always on grants. But he was looking for a position for himself. Somehow this-- his science was as specialized, let's say, as medicine is specialized. And he was accepted in Huntington, West Virginia in Marshall University, which is a state university. He is there the head of the clinic. He still does research in genetics. There are two or three students who he supervises, and they do research. And he teaches molecular biology medical students.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he married?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is married. He married in 1987. They have two children--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Stephanie Mara, Stephanie from after the name of my sister Zisel and Mara after Leo's sister Miriam. Also a year and a half later, she gave birth-- Deborah, our daughter in law, gave birth to Jared. His name is David after my brother David and Jared after Leo's brother Yakov from Lisko. The children are darling. They're very bright. Of course, they have got very bright parents who know how to bring them up and teach them. And I hope they'll be as good when they grow up as they are now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And grandparents, well thank you. Yes, what else would you like to know?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long are you and Mr. Finver married at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we married-- our [INAUDIBLE], ketubah was our ritual marriage, was made in Salzburg by that friend of ours, Abba Weinstein. He gave a rabbi a Red Cross package because we didn't even have money to pay him. And it was just 10 people came together with Abba and my brother, the two people that we knew in somebody else's house. They made a chuppah, and the rabbi gave us a ketubah, and that was it. When we came back to Bad Gastein, of course, we lived later on in Bad Gastein before we went to the United States. The consul-- we needed to have a certificate of marriage from the court. So we had a court certificate for Austria. But apparently the consul didn't think it was good enough. And we did it again. So our, our-- we celebrate now the 25th of May is the date of our marriage, the civil marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has your experience, your wartime experience, influenced the way you have lived your life during these 50 postwar years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, definitely nursing was one of mine, I would say dreams of my life that this would be my profession. Because when we came to the States, if we would have worked in the hotel, Leo and myself in any capacity as a little waitress, we could have had a much easier life, and we could have made more money. And perhaps we struggled financially at the beginning. But towards the end, we are satisfied it went the way it-- the way it went. Leo perhaps also, his brains and his ability and his photogenic memory is so phenomenal that he probably could have been an accountant. And he could have been a rabbi too because he had a very good Hebrew, Hebrew schooling at home. But all in all, I had very nice experiences working in hospitals. In Beth, Beth Israel, this was one of the nicest, even though I was a newcomer. But everybody was very nice. There was a group of nurses, as I said, Jewish nurses from Germany. And some were from Poland. And then when I came to Vineland, New Jersey, there only two or three Jewish nurses. They were mostly Catholic. It was a Catholic-- but also I had a very good experience, maybe because of my devotion to work, of my devotion to human beings, and because somehow we were brought up this way that you go to work. I didn't fail work. I didn't take sick days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the years that I worked, I worked all my years in the United States with interruptions when I was pregnant during the first time towards the end, and maybe two months after the first child died, I was too heart broken. Then I went back on-- in a nursery-- to a nursery. And when I was pregnant with Sheldon also, towards the end of the pregnancy I worked maybe two days only a week. Otherwise I worked my entire life in hospitals. Before we conclude, is there a message or a lesson that you would like to convey to your son and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that by our very, I would say-- how shall I say? Honorable is not exactly the word. Maybe a simple type, type of life, but our very honest and just hardworking type of life, and not running after money because we saw that during the war, the money was gone. That, I didn't have to tell my son. I think he is exactly the same. He is working with dedication on his job. He is-- if he would be a doctor, he would probably work for half the money and go to the poor people without any, any rewards. And he's bringing up, so far, the children, he and his wife-- who is a certified, certified nurse midwife-- also very nice. They are working people. And they lead also very honorable, clean life. That's all about I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as far-- as far as antisemitism, we came to the first Holocaust gathering. We came to Philadelphia because at that time I was able to take off from work and maybe Leo was semiretired. It must've been in '83 or '84. We came there. Our son was still in the university. And we asked him, Sheldon, shall we buy for you a ticket, because the tickets were $75 or $100 a person. Can we buy for you so you could come to the gathering? He said, no, thank you, because I am a volunteer. So without our telling us-- telling him of what went through of our past, he was instrumental in getting together, in making a second generation of Holocaust survivors in Philadelphia. And they, once a month, they made gathering. And each of them of the group was reading a chapter of the horrors that went through during the Holocaust, in Poland or in any other territory, and what Jews went through. So without our talking to him, he gave us book. He gave a Holocaust book written by an author, Gilbert-- he is a Englishman-- then we were astounded he wrote. Because every week, somebody else had to write-- read a chapter and saying to the gathered. So he, without really our, our being so withdrawn. Now we are talking more than we did in the past. Everybody was more preoccupied with making a living and perhaps not bringing on the pain or whatever. But anyhow, he is very well aware of what went on. He is very active in the Jewish circles, more than we ever anticipated or expected him to be. I would like to thank you for sharing your experiences with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thank you. This is really an experience that we will cherish forever. We have never spoken so openly and distinctly, especially about ours going away from the army because I sort of feel a little guilt. But the guilt is already gone a long time. As my friend Irene said, my input, my whatever, I had to contribute. I did contribute to German's defeat. And for Polish people, whatever I was there, I did for every wounded, whatever was needed, with a lot of devotion and a lot of love and care. And now of course I have no sentiment for Poland or for the Polish people because the way Irene said, it saturated the ground. It saturated with Jewish blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us who the gentleman is sitting next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Leon Finver, my husband. We manage in Austria in Bad Gastein March 8, 1946 by a rabbi. And our anniversary, the civil anniversary and civil papers, are from May 19-- 25, 1946. We were born in the same town, Lisko, but we met during the war under very unusual circumstances. And then our ways parted. And after the war Leo came to the place, to the hospital in which I was. And from there, we were together until now, and I hope we'll be for many more years together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sounds to me like you're ready to celebrate your 50th anniversary, this coming year-- this year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Finver, is there anything that you would like to say about Rita's wartime experiences and your being together?

speaker: interviewee
text: When it was her birthday, so I gave a public wish. This was a little celebration in our synagogue that we belonged. So I said this, many happier times together. The most important thing is together. We're not looking for any rewards. The only rewards will be in the future for us health and the things that we call in Hebrew nachas, nachas from the grandchildren. From the children we know already they're grown up. And we hope it will go on. Because at the climate that they have now in the United States, we're very much afraid for the young generation. But I hope everything will improve in the future. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you both again very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. You were very nice, both of you. I didn't think it was going to take that long. This is-- this is my paternal grandfather, Zalman Gershon Weilopolsky. He lived in Brasov. He died in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken sometimes in the '30s, maybe 1935, '36.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo was taken in Brasov. On the right side is my grandmother, Alta Raisel Manaster. The photo was taken in Lisko, Poland sometimes in 1936 or '37. On the left side is grandfather Leibish Manaster. This is the entire family. We lived in the same house. My aunt [INAUDIBLE]. My father-- I have to say [INAUDIBLE]. The photo was taken in front of our home. The date is 1938. This is a picture taken in Lisko by [NON-ENGLISH]. This is a school, a Jewish Hebrew school. The photo was taken sometimes in 1932, '33. This picture is taken in Lisko in one of the Zionist organizations. It's either Mizrahi or Hanoar. I am in the second row, the first from the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was a picture taken, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1933, 1934. I am here on the right hand side. The picture was taken in Katowice in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am with a friend who shared the room with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Rita Finver while in the Polish Army as a nurse.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1945, in Berlin, after the Germans were defeated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am sitting on the right side, the first row. The photo was taken in Rogoznica where our hospital was located.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in July, 1945. Rita Finver, first row, standing on the right side. This was taken in Bad Gastein in the clinic in 1946. Rita Finver. It was taken while I was in the hospital in the Polish Army sometimes in 1944. The first person is my brother, Abraham Weilopolsky. His name now is Manester. I am after him, Rita, married Finver. And on the left side is Leon Finver, my husband. The picture was taken in Bad Gastein, beginning 1946. On the right side I am here, Rita Finver. The second is our son, Sheldon Finver. On the left side is Leon Finver, my husband. The photo was taken in front of our house on a farm in Vineland, New Jersey-- in Newfield, New Jersey near Vineland. The picture was taken in 1954. Our son, Sheldon Finver, dressed for the graduation of students in Huntington, West Virginia. On the right side is his son, David Jared Finver. On the left is Stephanie Mara Finver.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Huntington, West Virginia in 1992. On the upper right, Rita Finver. The photo is taken in our house in Lakes of Carriage Hill, Tamarac, Florida. Next is our son, Sheldon Finver. On the left is Leon Finver. Below near the Hanukkah lights is our granddaughter, Stephanie Mara Finver. Next is our daughter in law, Deborah Albert Finver. And on the right side is our grandson, David Jared Finver. It, it was taken in 1992 or '93 in our home. This is an executive reward or award from Beth Abraham Hospital Montefiore Medical Center where I have spent 20 years as an RN.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked there from 1963 until 1985, with a small interruption to go to college.
